n.description: Number of vertices in the perfect graph

m.description: Number of edges in the perfect graph

E.description: Edges of the graph; each row lists the two endpoint vertices of an edge, with the smaller vertex index listed first

P.description: Number of disjoint clusters partitioning the vertex set

C.description: Binary cluster membership matrix: C_{i,p} is 1 if vertex i belongs to cluster p, 0 otherwise

assumptions.description: The number of vertices is positive.
The number of edges is non-negative.
Each cluster contains at least one vertex.

code.python: assert all(sum(C[i][p] for i in range(n)) >= 1 for p in range(P))

assumptions.description: The clusters partition the vertex set: every vertex belongs to exactly one cluster.

code.python: assert all(sum(C[i][p] for p in range(P)) == 1 for i in range(n))

assumptions.description: Every edge connects two distinct, valid vertex indices.

code.python: assert all(0 <= i < j <= n - 1 for i, j in E)

assumptions.description: The graph is perfect: every induced subgraph has chromatic number equal to its clique number.

x.description: x_i equals 1 if vertex i is selected, 0 otherwise

t.description: Estimate of the number of colors needed to color the selected vertices

constraints.description: Exactly one vertex is selected from each cluster.

code.gurobipy:
for p in range(P):
    model.addConstr(gp.quicksum(C[i][p] * x[i] for i in range(n)) == 1)

constraints.description: t is at least the number of selected vertices within any clique of the graph; since the graph is perfect, the largest such count over all cliques equals the chromatic number of the induced subgraph on the selected vertices.

code.gurobipy:
G_temp = nx.Graph()
G_temp.add_nodes_from(range(n))
G_temp.add_edges_from(E)
for clique in nx.find_cliques(G_temp):
    model.addConstr(t >= gp.quicksum(x[i] for i in clique))

constraints.description: t is no more than P.

objective.description: Minimize the number of colors needed to color the selected vertices.

notes: Instead of having t be a continuous, only-lower-bounded relaxation variable (justified there by the iterative cutting-plane algorithm's LP relaxations), we assert that t is always driven to an integer value at any optimum, and is bounded above by P for every feasible selection (since at most P vertices are ever selected, one per cluster). We therefore additionally restrict t to be integer-valued and cap it at P; this removes only fractional or unnecessarily-large feasible values of t and does not exclude the true optimum or change the optimal objective value.
